coredata.dc:title: Circular dichroism glucose biosensor based on chiral cadmium sulfide quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ngamdee, Kessarin
Ngeontae, Wittaya
coredata.dc:description: A new approach to the fabrication of a glucose biosensor based on circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD) using cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) as a sensor probe was successfully developed and showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity. The chiral CdS QDs (DPA/Cys-CdS QDs) can be simply prepared by mixing cysteamine-capped CdS QDs (Cys-CdS QDs; achiral QDs) with D-penicillamine (DPA). The as-prepared DPA/Cys-CdS QDs are active in circular dichroism spectroscopy due to the chirality of DPA. The principle of glucose detection is based on the destruction of chiral QDs by the H2O2 generated in situ from the enzymatic reaction of glucose oxidase (GOx) and glucose in the presence of dissolved oxygen. In the presence of the generated H2O2, the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs were etched, and the CD signal decreased as a linear function of the glucose concentration. This sensor provided excellent selectivity for the detection of glucose over other possible interfering compounds because of the combination of the enzymatic reaction and the circular dichroism measurement. Parameters that might affect the detection sensitivity of the sensor were investigated and optimized. Under the optimized conditions, the linear working concentration range was found to be 50–250 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 31 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical sensors
Glucose biosensors
Quantum dots
Circular dichroism spectroscopy
Glucose oxidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of glucose in coconut water sample by HPLC
Application of the proposed sensor in real samples
Comparison of the figure merits of the proposed sensor with other sensing platforms

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Office of the Higher Education Commission
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Office of the Higher Education Commission

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund ( RSA6080006 and RTA6080005 ) and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Office of the Higher Education Commission, Ministry of Education .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of the glucose biosensor based on chiral CdS QDs and detection by CD spectroscopy.

CD spectra of GOx, Cys-CdS QDs, and DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence of H2O2, of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the presence of glucose, and of GOx/DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence of glucose in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5. (Conditions: [DPA] = 100 μM, [Cys-CdS QDs] = 112.5 μg mL−1, [GOx] = 3.0 U mL−1, [H2O2] = 175 μM, [glucose] = 200 μM).

(a) CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM of DPA mixed with 112.5 μg mL−1 of Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing 3.0 U mL−1 GOx in the absence and presence of 200 μM of glucose in 50 mM buffer solution and the corresponding CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose. (b) The corresponding CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at 253 nm as a function of solution pH. (c) The degree of decrease in the CD signal as a function of solution pH. (d) CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing GOx at different concentrations of DPA in the range of 0–125 μM in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose. (e) The corresponding CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at 253 nm as a function of DPA concentration. (f) The degree of decrease in the CD signal as a function of DPA concentration. (g) CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM DPA mixed with various concentrations of Cys-CdS QDs in the range of 0–150 μg mL−1) in the system containing 3.0 U mL−1 GOx in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 in the absence and presence of 200 μM of glucose. (h) The CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at 253 nm as a function of Cys-CdS QDs concentration. (i) The degree of decrease in the CD signal as a function of Cys-CdS QDs concentration.

a) CD signal at 253 nm of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM DPA mixed with 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing 3.0 U mL−1 GOx in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 as a function of incubation time in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose. (b) The degree of decrease in the CD signal as a function of incubation time.

a) CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM DPA mixed with 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing various concentration of GOx in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose. (b) The corresponding CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at 253 nm. (c) The degree of decrease in the CD signal.

a) CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM DPA mixed with 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing 3.0 U mL-1 GOx in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 in the absence and presence of 200 μM concentrations of different types of sugar. (b) The corresponding CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence of 200 μM of glucose at 253 nm. (c) The corresponding degree of decrease in the CD signal.

CD spectra of (a) DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (b) LPA/Cys-CdS QDs (100 μM DPA or LPA mixed with 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs) in the system containing 3.0 U mL−1 GOx in 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 in the absence and presence of different glucose concentrations. (c) The corresponding calibration curve from the CD signal at 253 nm.
Determination of glucose in coconut water sample by the proposed sensor and by the HPLC method.
Comparison of the merits of glucose sensors fabricated from other sensing platforms.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Abbreviation: CdTe/CdS QDs, CdTe/CdS quantum dots; QD-Au NP, Quantum dot-Au nanoparticle; GQDs/AgNPs, Graphene quantum dots/silver nanoparticles; N-GQDs, Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots; JFSNs, Janus γ-Fe2O3/SiO2 nanoparticles; β-CD-ZnS QDs, β-cyclodextrin modified ZnS-quantum dots; MoS2 QDs, Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots; Pt NCs, Pt nanoclusters; C-dots/AgNPs, Carbon nanodots supported on silver nanoparticles; C-dots/AgNPs, Multielement-doped carbon dots; Co2O3/Co3O4 NPs, Cobalt oxides nanoparticles; Ag-CuO/rGO, Ag-CuO nanoparticles on reduced graphene oxide; CuO@Cu/g-C3N4, Copper oxide@copper/Carbon nitride nanosheets; AgNPs, Silver nanoparticles; DPA/Cys-CdS QDs; d-penicillamine/cysteamine-capped CdS quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Circular dichroism glucose biosensor based on chiral cadmium sulfide quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Chemistry Research Center, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen 40002, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new approach to the fabrication of a glucose biosensor based on circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD) using cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) as a sensor probe was successfully developed and showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity. The chiral CdS QDs (DPA/Cys-CdS QDs) can be simply prepared by mixing cysteamine-capped CdS QDs (Cys-CdS QDs; achiral QDs) with D-penicillamine (DPA). The as-prepared DPA/Cys-CdS QDs are active in circular dichroism spectroscopy due to the chirality of DPA. The principle of glucose detection is based on the destruction of chiral QDs by the H2O2 generated in situ from the enzymatic reaction of glucose oxidase (GOx) and glucose in the presence of dissolved oxygen. In the presence of the generated H2O2, the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs were etched, and the CD signal decreased as a linear function of the glucose concentration. This sensor provided excellent selectivity for the detection of glucose over other possible interfering compounds because of the combination of the enzymatic reaction and the circular dichroism measurement. Parameters that might affect the detection sensitivity of the sensor were investigated and optimized. Under the optimized conditions, the linear working concentration range was found to be 50–250 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 31 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Circular dichroism spectroscopy (CD) is an important technique used to examine the absolute chirality of molecules. The detection principle of CD is based on measuring the difference in absorption between left- and right-handed circularly polarized light [1]. Recently, circular dichroism sensors based on chiral nanomaterials have been proposed [2–4]. This approach can provide excellent selectivity due to the specific interaction/reaction between the sensor probe and the target analyte. Moreover, good selectivity can be obtained by the measurement of the circular dichroism phenomenon, which is subject to fewer interfering species than other techniques. For these reasons, the development of chemical sensors based on circular dichroism spectroscopy is currently of interest. Several types of interaction/reaction with chiral nanomaterials have been introduced, such as the formation of a chiral complex [2] and the in situ generation of chiral nanomaterials from achiral nanomaterials [5,6].

Several types of chiral nanomaterials have been reported, such as gold nanorods [7,8], silver nanoparticles [9,10], and quantum dots [11–13]. Most reports on the subject have described the synthesis and chiroptical properties of the as-prepared compounds. However, applications of the synthesized chiral nanomaterials in the fabrication of circular dichroism sensors have rarely been reported. Chiral quantum dots are among the most interesting chiral nanomaterials. Basically, chiral molecules provide a strong CD signal in the UV range but essentially no signal in the visible region [14]. Chirality in semiconductor quantum dots can be obtained by ligand-induced chirality of the QD surface [12], by electronic coupling between chiral capping ligands and achiral QDs, or by the formation of chiral assemblies of achiral QDs [6].

Accordingly, the optical chirality of nanosystems is an interesting area of research that has received much attention [15]. Many researchers are interested in the utilization of specific chiral molecules as capping agents for the fabrication of new sensing probes [2,4,16–18]. Very recently, the fabrication of the circular dichroism sensors based on the chiroptical properties of chiral quantum dots have been reported by our research group. L-cysteine-capped cadmium sulfide quantum dots (l-Cyst-CdS QDs) were proposed as chiral quantum dots to be used as sensor probes for the selective determination of Ni2+ and Co2+ by monitoring the circular dichroism signals [2]. This report is the first to state that CD spectroscopy can be used for the determination of trace metal ions. Another report describes the fabrication of a sensor for the chiral identification and quantitative analysis of penicillamine by using quantum dots as a sensor probe and monitoring by CD spectroscopy [6].

Glucose is an important source of energy for biological processes. The detection of glucose is therefore of great importance in food analysis as well as in the identification of metabolites [19]. It is well known that glucose can be oxidized by oxygen (O2) in the presence of glucose oxidase (GOx), resulting in the generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Many glucose biosensors are based on the detection of the H2O2 generated by this reaction by using several detection principles, including both direct and indirect approaches, such as electrochemical sensors [20,21], colorimetric sensors [22,23], fluorescence sensors [24,25], and conductance glucose biosensors [26]. Various nanomaterials have been proposed for the fabrication of sensor probes to detect the H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction of glucose [26–28]. The generated H2O2 can cause oxidative stress through the oxidation of the fluorescent nanomaterials. In the literature, most reports of the utilization of nanomaterials to detect the H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction of glucose are based on the measurement of the fluorescence quenching. However, there have not been any reports on the utilization of circular dichroism spectroscopy to detect the H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction for glucose determination.

In this work, we have proposed for the first time a circular dichroism biosensor for the detection of glucose by the facile in situ generation of chiral quantum dots (DPA/Cys-CdS QDs) from achiral quantum dots (Cys-CdS QDs). The chiral sensor probe is responsible for the detection of H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction of GOx and glucose. By coupling the enzymatic reaction with specific detection by the circular dichroism activity of the sensor probe, a highly sensitive and selective biosensor for the detection of glucose can be obtained. Moreover, the feasibility of the proposed sensor in practical application is demonstrated by the determination of the glucose content in coconut water samples and comparison of the results obtained by the proposed sensor and by HPLC.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Cysteamine hydrochloride was purchased from Sigma. Glucose oxidase from Aspergillus niger (GOx, EC 1.1.3.4, 173.5 U mg−1), glucose, l-penicillamine (LPA) and d-penicillamine (DPA) were purchased from Aldrich. Sodium sulfide was obtained from BDH. Cadmium chloride (CdCl2·H2O), potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate and di-potassium hydrogen orthophosphate were obtained from UNIVAR. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm obtained from a RiOs ™ Type I Simplicity 185 (Millipore Water). The cysteamine-capped CdS quantum dots (Cys-CdS QDs) were synthesized and characterized using the procedure published previously [29] as detailed in the electronic supporting materials (ESI).

Circular dichroism spectra were recorded with a Jasco-815 CD spectropolarimeter (JASCO, Japan) using a 1 cm quartz cell cuvette with a scanning rate of 200 nm min−1. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer. Excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a slit width of 5 nm. Absorption spectra were measured using an Agilent HP 8453 spectrophotometer. The pH of solutions was determined by using a UB-10 UltraBasic pH meter (Denver Instrument). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded with a JEOL JEM 1230 TEM (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) under an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were recorded by operating at a 100 kV accelerating voltage.

In a 10.00 mL volumetric flask containing a solution of glucose at the given concentration, 75 μL of 15 mg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs (to obtain a final concentration of 112.5 μg mL−1) was mixed with 100 μL of 10.0 mM DPA (to obtain a final concentration of 100 μM), 200 μL of 150 U mL−1 GOx (to obtain a final concentration of 3.0 U mL−1) and 500 μL of 1.0 M Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5. The mixture was then brought to a final volume of 10.00 mL with deionized water. The resulting solution mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 60 min. The CD signal at a wavelength of 253 nm was used for quantitative analysis.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensors, the following procedure was carried out. In a 10.00 mL volumetric flask containing different types of individual sugar solution at a final concentration of −200 μM, 75 μL of 15 mg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs was mixed with 100 μL of 10.0 mM DPA, 200 μL of 150 U mL−1 GOx and 500 μL of 1.0 M Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5. The mixture was then brought to a final volume of 10.00 mL with deionized water. The solution was mixed thoroughly and allowed to stand for 60 min before recording the CD spectra.

To show the feasibility of the proposed method and the applicability of the proposed sensor in real samples, a coconut water sample was used. The coconut water samples were filtered through filter paper (11 μm pore size) to remove particulate matter and diluted 20 fold with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer pH 8.5. In a 10.00 mL volumetric flask, 75 μL of 15 mg mL−1 Cyst-CdS QDs was mixed with 100 μL of 10.0 mM DPA, 200 μL of 150 U mL−1 GOx and 500 μL of 1.0 M Tris-HCl buffer solution pH 8.5. The mixture was then brought to a final volume of 10.00 mL with the diluted sample and equilibrated for 60 min at room temperature. The accuracy and precision of the measurement were evaluated by spiking the sample solution with a standard glucose solution at 50 and 100 μM before measuring the concentration of glucose by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose in coconut water sample by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method, the glucose concentrations in the real samples and the corresponding spiked samples were also determined by HPLC. The HPLC (LC20 A, Shimadzu, Japan) experiments were performed using the following conditions. An Inertsil ODS-3 250 × 4.60 mm column (GL Science Inc., Japan) was equipped with an LC-20AD pump system and a refractive index detector. The mobile phase was 100% water. The flow rate of the mobile phase was 1.0 mL min−1, and the injection volume was 20 μL. The glucose in the samples and the corresponding spiked samples was identified by comparison with the retention time of standards, and the concentrations of glucose were determined using the external standard method.

This work aims to demonstrate a new strategy for the fabrication of an enzyme biosensor by using the special optical property of the chiral cadmium sulfide quantum dots shown in Scheme 1 . The change in the chiral cadmium sulfide quantum dots based on the enzymatic reaction was recorded by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Excellence selectivity can be expected for this strategy because of the combination of the excellent properties of CD spectroscopy and the bio-enzymatic reaction. The sensitivity of the sensor was studied, optimized and discussed in detail.

The sensor probe was designed by using Cys-CdS QDs, which is achiral QDs that are not active in CD spectroscopy. However, the QDs can be easily generated by the addition of a chiral thiol-containing compound such as DPA or LPA. It is well known that a rapid reaction between CdS QDs and the thiol moiety (SH) can occur via the formation of the CdS bond [30]. Thus, it is possible to generate chiral CdS QDs by this strategy. In this work, DPA was used to modify the Cys-CdS QDs to generate a chiral probe for detection by CD spectroscopy. The results in Fig. 1 clearly show that no CD signal could be recorded from the Cys-CdS QDs solution. On the other hand, strong CD signals at wavelengths of 230, 253 and 265 nm can be measured upon the addition of DPA to the solution of Cys-CdS QDs. This result confirms that chiral CdS QDs were generated, as we reported previously [6]. It should be noted that the CD spectrum of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs was wholly different from the CD spectrum of DPA itself in both pattern and intensity.

It is well known that an enzymatic reaction is a highly specific reaction between an enzyme and substrate, such as glucose oxidase (GOx) and glucose. The reaction between GOx and glucose (in the presence of O2) yields H2O2 as a by-product. The concentration of glucose is related to the amount of H2O2 generated. This reaction allows the selective detection of glucose over other sugar types. Because H2O2 is an oxidizing agent, the generated H2O2 can be expected to oxidize the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs. A decrease in the degree of chirality should be expected after the reaction with H2O2, and the CD signal should be decreased as a result. Thus, combining the enzymatic reaction with a selective chemical sensor such as the CD sensor is therefore an interesting strategy for the fabrication of excellent biosensors.

To prove this concept, experiments were carried out, and the results are shown in Fig. 1. The CD spectrum of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the presence of GOx (at the working concentration) was measured. The result showed that there was no significant change in either the CD signal or the spectrum shape upon the addition of GOx. This result revealed that it is possible to incorporate GOx into the detection system. However, the CD intensity was dramatically decreased in the presence of glucose. This decrease was due to the generation of H2O2 from the enzymatic reaction and the reaction of the generated H2O2 with the sensor probe to decrease the degree of chirality. This phenomenon proved that the enzymatic reaction occurred and directly affected the CD signal. Thus, a glucose biosensor can be fabricated by measuring the degree of the decrease in the CD signal. To confirm the role of GOx, the equivalent experiment was performed without GOx. The result strongly confirmed that there was no change in the CD signal upon the addition of glucose to the system without GOx. From this experiment, it can be concluded that the detection system must be composed of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs and GOx. The decrease in the CD signal can be observed only upon the addition of glucose to the detection system.

The decrease in the CD signal upon the addition of glucose to the detection system was studied in detail. It is known that H2O2 can be generated from the enzymatic reaction between GOx and glucose in the presence of oxygen as a co-factor. Because the generated H2O2 is an oxidizing agent, it can be expected to destroy the sensing probe (DPA/Cys-CdS QDs). This destruction results in a decrease in the amount of the sensing probe and a decrease in the degree of chirality of the QDs. To prove that the decrease in the CD signal was a result of the generated H2O2, the corresponding experiment was performed by adding H2O2 directly to the sensor probe, and the results are shown in Fig. S1 (ESI). The results clearly demonstrated that a dramatic decreased in the CD signal can be observed upon the addition of H2O2 to the sensor probe, which is similar to the results of the addition of glucose to the mixture of GOx and DPA/Cys-CdS QDs. This result strongly confirmed that the decrease in the CD signal was due to the reaction between H2O2 and the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs.

To confirm the reaction between the generated H2O2 and the sensor probe, TEM images of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (in the presence of GOx) before and after adding glucose were obtained to observe the morphology of the sensor probe, and the results are shown in Fig. S2 (ESI). The TEM images of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs showed that the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs were nearly spherical particles with a narrow size distribution and an average diameter of 3.8 ± 0.5 nm. On the other hand, the average diameter of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs decreased significantly to approximately 1.7 ± 0.4 nm after the addition of glucose. These results confirmed that DPA/Cys-CdS QDs were destroyed due to the enzymatic reaction between GOx and glucose, causing the decrease in the CD signal.

In addition, the change in the size of the sensor probe due to the enzymatic reaction was further investigated by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique, and the results are shown in Fig. S3 (ESI). It should be noted that the particle sizes obtained from the DLS technique are all larger than those obtained from the TEM experiment. This difference in size is because the DLS technique measures the hydrodynamic size of the suspended particles. The results demonstrated that the particle size of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the presence of GOx and 25 μM of glucose significantly decreased from the size in the system without glucose. In addition, the depletion of the particle can be observed when the concentration of glucose is further increased to 50 μM. This decrease may be due to the complete destruction of the sensor probe by the enzymatic reaction. The results obtained from DLS also corresponded well to the TEM results.

Basically, the CdS QDs are sensitive to changes to the core or capping molecule. Thus, if the H2O2 generated from enzymatic reaction interacts with the core CdS QDs, the fluorescent spectrum of the Cys-CdS QDs must be affected. The fluorescence spectra of Cys-CdS QDs mixed with GOx in the absence and presence of glucose were therefore investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S4 (ESI). The fluorescence intensity of the Cys-CdS QDs decreased linearly with the increase in the concentration of glucose once the concentration of glucose was higher than 50 μM. This result revealed that the Cys-CdS QDs were destroyed by the generated H2O2, decreasing the number of fluorescent species. It can be seen that glucose detection can be established by both fluorescence and CD techniques by using Cys-CdS QDs as sensor probe for the same enzymatic reaction. However, the detection sensitivity and selectivity obtained from the CD technique seemed to be superior to those obtained by the fluorescence technique.

The above evidence clearly indicates that the decrease in the CD signal occurred because the H2O2 generated by the enzymatic reaction destroyed the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs. The degree of decrease in the CD signal can be directly related to the concentration of glucose. Thus, it is possible to fabricate a new type of glucose biosensor based on CD spectroscopy by using chiral QDs in situ generated. The parameters that might affect to the detection sensitivity were studied and optimized as detailed in the following section.

Basically, the enzyme activity is always affected by the solution pH. Therefore, the pH of the working assay must be studied and optimized. To evaluate the optimum pH, phosphate buffer solution was used to control the pH of the solution in the range of 6.0–7.0, and Tris-HCl buffer was used for the pH range of 7.5–9.5. The CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (mixed with GOx) in the absence and presence of glucose at different solution pH values are illustrated in Fig. 2 (a–c). The results showed that the signal of the sensor probe cannot be detected at a solution pH lower than 7.0. In contrast, the CD signals of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (mixed with GOx) were significantly increased at solution pH values higher than 7.0. This behavior occurred because the binding between the thiol group of DPA and Cd2+ on the surface of Cys-CdS QDs can be affected by the solution pH. The formation of the SCd bond (thiol group of DPA and CdS core QDs) is more favorable at neutral pH than at acidic pH. This result suggested that chiral CdS QDs can be generated from achiral CdS QDs in basic solution. After adding glucose to the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (mixed with GOx), a decrease in CD signals can be observed at all studied solution pH values. This result indicated that the enzymatic reaction between GOx and glucose can occur throughout the studied pH range. However, the highest sensitivity was obtained at pH 8.5. Therefore, the pH of 8.5 was selected for use in further studies.

As DPA is a key compound for the successful generation of chiral CdS QDs from achiral QDs, the intensity of the CD signal depends on the amount of DPA in the assay. Because the measurement relies on a decrease in the CD signal, the initial CD intensity is a crucial parameter that may affect the sensor sensitivity. Thus, the effect of DPA concentration was studied and optimized. The experiment was carried out by varying the concentration of DPA in the range of 0–125 μM with the solution pH controlled at 8.5 with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer. The CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs (mixed with GOx) at different concentrations of DPA in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose were measured and are shown in Fig. 2(d–f). In the absence of glucose, the CD signal increased with increasing DPA concentration (Fig. 2(d) and (e)) due to the increasing number of chiral CdS QDs. On the other hand, the CD signal decreased for all studied DPA concentration in the presence of 200 μM glucose. The degree of decrease in the CD signal (CD-CD0) is shown in Fig. 2(f). This result showed that the number of chiral CdS QDs can affect the detection sensitivity, but an excessive number of sensor probes is not necessarily required. The highest detection sensitivity was obtained when using 100 μM DPA, and this concentration was chosen for use in further studies.

The Cys-CdS QDs are one of the essential components for the fabrication of this sensor. The Cys-CdS QDs are not active in CD spectroscopy because they lack any chiroptical property. However, they are crucial for the generation of chiral CdS QDs. Thus, the effect of Cys-CdS QDs concentration on the detection sensitivity was studied and optimized by varying the Cys-CdS QDs concentration in the range of 0–150.0 μg mL−1 and controlling the solution pH at 8.5 with 50 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution. The CD signals of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at different Cys-CdS QDs concentrations in the absence and presence of 200 μM glucose are displayed in Fig. 2(g). In the absence of glucose, the CD signal, as shown in Fig. 2(h), increased as the concentration of Cys-CdS QDs increased due to the increasing number of chiral CdS QDs in the system. On the other hand, the CD signal of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the system containing GOx decreased for all studied Cys-CdS QDs concentrations in the presence of 200 μM glucose. The degrees of change in the CD signal (CD-CD0) at different Cys-CdS QDs concentrations are compared in Fig. 2(i). The results showed that the degree of change in the CD signal increased with increasing Cys-CdS QDs concentration from 0 to 112.5 μg mL−1 and decreased beyond this level. The highest detection sensitivity was obtained when using 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs, and therefore, this concentration was chosen for further experiments. The results of this experiment showed that the concentration of Cys-CdS QDs used for the fabrication of the sensor has a significant effect on the detection sensitivity.

To achieve a compromise between the detection sensitivity and the analysis time, the effect of the incubation time was also examined. The experiment was carried out by mixing 100 μM DPA with 112.5 μg mL−1 Cys-CdS QDs and 3.0 U mL−1 GOx in 50 mM Tris–HCl buffer solution pH 8.5 at room temperature for a period of 75 min and recording the CD spectra every 5 min. The CD signals of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs at 253 nm as a function of incubation time are shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that the CD signal of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs itself was very stable throughout the period of the study. After the enzymatic reaction between GOx and glucose, the CD signal decreased as a function of incubation time because the enzymatic reaction is a time-dependent reaction. The accumulation of the H2O2 generated from the enzymatic reaction, which corresponded to the degree of change in the CD spectra, affected the detection sensitivity. The degree of change in the CD signal, as shown in Fig. 3(b), showed that the detection sensitivity can be increased by increasing the incubation time. The measurement can be performed after incubating for 20 min with the lowest detection sensitivity. However, as a compromise between sensitivity and analysis time, the incubation time of 60 min was chosen for further study.

Basically, in an enzymatic reaction, if the concentration of the substrate is to be quantified, the concentration of enzyme must be greater than that of the substrate so that the rate of the reaction will be dependent on the concentration of the substrate. To achieve glucose determination with the highest detection sensitivity, the concentration of GOx was therefore studied. The experiment was carried out by fixing the glucose concentration at 200 μM and varying the concentration of GOx from 0 to 3.75 U mL−1. The CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence of glucose were recorded, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The results in Fig. 4(a) and (b) showed that in the absence of glucose, the CD signal of the sensor probe was very stable as the concentration of GOx increased. This stability signified the compatibility between the sensor probe and GOx and showed that GOx can be used at any concentration level without interference from the presence of the enzyme. Moreover, in the presence of glucose, when the concentration of the enzyme was less than 2.25 U mL−1, the reaction rate was dependent on the enzyme concentration, as shown by the results in Fig. 4(c). This phenomenon always occurs when the concentration of the enzyme is lower than that of the substrate. On the other hand, when the concentration of the enzyme was increased from 2.25 to 3.75 U mL−1, the degree of change in the CD signal showed no significant difference. This result signified that the reaction rate was dependent on the substrate concentration. Therefore, the GOx concentration of 3.0 U mL−1 was chosen as the working enzyme concentration for the studied glucose concentration range.

Basically, the selectivity of the chemical sensor is determined by the specific interactions or reactions between the receptor and its target analyte. However, the selectivity of the chemical sensor is still a challenging issue. The selectivity of this sensor was designed by combining the dominant characteristics of the CD sensor with those of the enzyme biosensor. As CD spectroscopy can detect only chiroptical compounds, other compounds with no chiroptical properties will not alter the CD signal. In addition, the selectivity of the sensor is results from the specific reaction between H2O2 and the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs. However, the CD signal may suffer interference from other oxidizing agents in the same reaction as H2O2. Thus, if the H2O2 is generated from a specific reaction such as the enzymatic reaction between GOx and glucose, the selectivity of the detection should be much improved. Therefore, coupling the advantages of the CD sensor with those of the enzymatic biosensor should provide a highly selective sensor.

The selectivity of the sensor for glucose detection over other sugar types including fructose, maltose, lactose sucrose and xylose was studied. The CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the absence and presence different types of sugar are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The corresponding CD signal and the degree of change in the CD signal are shown in Fig. 5(b) and (c), respectively. Interestingly, the CD signal was decreased only in the presence of glucose, while other types of sugar did not significantly alter the original probe signal. This result signified that the proposed sensor possesses high selectivity for the detection of glucose due to the combination of the CD sensor with the enzymatic glucose biosensor.

The feasibility of the proposed sensor for quantitative analysis was demonstrated by constructing the calibration curve. In addition, to show the possibility of using another isomer of the chiral CdS QDs in quantitative analysis, the calibration curve was constructed for another type of chiral CdS QDs. The analogous chiral CdS QDs were generated in situ by using LPA instead of DPA under the optimized conditions for DPA. The CD spectra of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs and LPA/Cys-CdS QDs (mixed with GOx) at different concentrations of glucose are shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), respectively. Upon increase of the glucose concentration from 0 to 250 μM, the CD signal decreased linearly as a function of the increase in the glucose concentration. It should be noted that mirror image spectra can be observed at each glucose concentration level with the use of DPA/Cys-CdS QDs and LPA/Cys-CdS QDs as the sensor probe. This result signified that the chemistry of the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs and LPA/Cys-CdS QDs in the detection of glucose is not different; only the direction of the polarized light is opposite. It is not necessary to use the D- isomer, as the L- isomer can also be applied with the same sensor response (with the opposite direction of polarized light). The degree of decrease in the CD signal (CD-CD0) as a function of glucose concentration is shown in Fig. 6(c). The regression equation was found to be CD-CD0 = 0.78×[glucose, μM] − 21.44 (R2 = 0.9856) and CD-CD0 = −0.73×[glucose, μM] −+ 29.91 (R2 = 0.9860) when using DPA/Cys-CdS QDs and LPA/Cys-CdS QDs as the sensor probe, respectively. It was clearly seen that the detection sensitivity obtained from the two sensor probes did not differ significantly. Moreover, the important validation parameters were evaluated to show the merit of the proposed sensor. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were determined to show the limitations of the sensor. The LOD was calculated as the concentration of glucose giving a degree of CD decrease (CD-CD0) equal to 3× the standard deviation of CD0, while the LOQ was measured as the glucose concentration that provided a degree of CD decrease (CD-CD0) equal to 10× the standard deviation of the CD0 by using DPA/Cys-CdS QDs as the sensor probe. The LOD and LOQ of the proposed sensor were then calculated to be 31 and 38 μM, respectively. Prof. Meyerhoff and co-worker have been reported the LOQs of the commercial glucometer strips from several manufacturers [31]. The LOQ of the test strip based electrochemical detection are in the range of 45–2040 μM. However, the LOQ of the proposed sensor can be achieved at 38 μM signified that the proposed sensor is one of the sensitive method for detection of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the proposed sensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the application of the proposed sensor for the detection of glucose in a real situation, a coconut water sample was used as a model. To check the recovery obtained, a standard glucose solution was spiked into the coconut water sample at concentrations of 50.0 and 100.0 μM. The concentration of the glucose in the samples and the corresponding spiked samples were calculated according to the calibration curve constructed from a DI water medium. In addition, to confirm the accuracy of the proposed sensor, the spiked samples were also determined by HPLC. The obtained glucose concentration in the coconut water samples and the corresponding spiked samples are summarized in Table 1 . Excellent recoveries were found in the range from 100 to 103% with relative standard deviations (%RSD) lower than 5.0%, suggesting that the proposed sensor provided good precision. Moreover, the glucose concentrations in the sample and the corresponding spiked samples agreed well with the results from the HPLC method, which strongly confirmed the accuracy of the proposed sensor as well. This experiment shows that the proposed sensor was reliable and can be utilized in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the figure merits of the proposed sensor with other sensing platforms
ce:section.ce:para: Several sensing platforms have been proposed for the detection of glucose which is summarized in Table 2 . It can be seen that there are various types of sensing platforms including UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and electrochemistry have been proposed. Most of the sensing platforms utilized the special properties of nanomaterials including carbon based materials, metal nanoparticles, and nanocrystalline semiconductor quantum dots for fabrication of the sensors. The reported methods provided a narrow working concentration range with relatively low detection limits at a sub micro-molar level. Although the detection limit of this method is not significantly lower than that found in the previous reports, it is the same order of magnitude. However, its selectivity due to measurement of circular dichroism signal by using CdS QDs coupled with enzymatic reaction is quite remarkable.

A new biosensor platform for highly selective glucose detection based on circular dichroism spectroscopy was successfully developed by using in situ generated CdS QDs as a sensor probe. The sensor probe can be simply prepared by using achiral CdS QDs (Cys-CdS QDs) mixed with a chiral thiol-containing compound (DPA) to form the Cd-S bond. The generated chiral CdS QDs (DPA/Cys-CdS QDs) are active in the CD spectroscopy. The CD signal of the sensor probe was highly sensitive to H2O2. In the presence of H2O2, the DPA/Cys-CdS QDs were destroyed, and a decrease in the CD signal could be observed. This phenomenon allowed the detection of glucose by indirect measurement of the concentration of H2O2 generated by the enzymatic reaction of GOx and glucose. The concentration of glucose is directly related to the degree of decrease in the CD signal with a wide working linear concentration range and a low detection limit. Remarkable selectivity of the proposed sensor for the detection of glucose over other related compounds can be achieved. The proposed biosensor platform can also potentially be applied for the development and fabrication of other biosensors or chemical sensors related to H2O2 in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund (RSA6080006 and RTA6080005) and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Office of the Higher Education Commission, Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kessarin Ngamdee was a Ph.D. student in Chemistry under the supervision of associate professor Wittaya Ngeontae, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. She got her Master Degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2011 from Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. Her research interest is the fabrication of new optical sensors.

Wittaya Ngeontae is currently an associate professor in Department of Chemistry at Khon Kaen University, Khon Kaen, Thailand. He earned his Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry in 2008 from Chulalongkorn University under the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology Talent project (DPST). He was the Thailand’s Young Scientist Award in 2013. His current research interests include chemical sensors based optical nanomaterials.
